THE TEMPLE SCROLL -- 11Q19 -- COLUMNS 45 THROUGH 66

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 45 The Laws of the King — He Shall Not Multiply Horses — He Shall Not Multiply Wives — He Shall Not Multiply Silver and Gold — He Shall Write for Himself a Copy of the Torah

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 When you enter the land which Creator your Creator gives you and possess it and dwell in it, and you say I will set a king over me like all the nations around me —

2 You shall set a king over you whom Creator your Creator will choose. From among your brothers you shall set a king over you — you may not put a foreigner who is not your brother over you.

3 Only he must not acquire many horses for himself or cause the people to return to Egypt in order to acquire horses — since Creator has said to you: You shall never return that way again.

4 And he shall not acquire many wives for himself lest his heart turn away. And silver and gold he shall not acquire in great quantity for himself.

5 And when he sits on the throne of his kingdom he shall write for himself in a book a copy of this Torah from before the priests the Levites.

6 And it shall be with him and he shall read in it all the days of his life that he may learn to fear Creator his Creator by keeping all the words of this Torah and these statutes and doing them.

7 That his heart may not be lifted up above his brothers and that he may not turn aside from the commandment to the right hand or to the left.

8 So that he may continue long in his kingdom, he and his children, in the midst of Yisra'EL.

9 And he shall not multiply horses to himself — I am the Creator who redeemed you from Egypt — therefore you shall not return that way again.

10 And he shall not take a wife from among the daughters of the nations but from his father's house, from his father's family shall he take a wife for himself.

11 He shall not take another wife in addition to her — she alone shall be with him all the days of her life.

12 And if she dies he may take another wife from his father's family and from his father's house — but he shall not take two wives at the same time.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 45 contains the Temple Scroll's Law of the King which expands Devarim 17:14-20. The strict monogamy requirement of verses 11-12 is the Temple Scroll's most significant addition to the Masoretic king legislation. The Damascus Document applies the same covenant monogamy principle to all community members. The king's example of covenant faithfulness in marriage grounds the entire community's covenant marriage standard. The Temple Scroll's king is not merely a political leader — he is the covenant example whose personal life models the standard Creator requires of all his people.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 17:14-20 and the full covenant history of the kings in Melakhim. The Orit Ge'ez kings who kept the Torah of the King — particularly Dawid and Yoshiyahu — are the historical models for the ideal king of Column 45. The kings who violated these provisions — particularly the horse multiplication of Shelomoh (Melakhim Aleph 10:26-29) and his wife multiplication (Melakhim Aleph 11:1-8) — are the historical warnings that the Temple Scroll's legislation is designed to prevent.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 17:14-20 provides the base text. The Temple Scroll significantly expands with the foreign wife prohibition (verse 10), the strict monogamy requirement (verses 11-12), and the repeated horse prohibition with theological rationale (verse 9). These additions reflect the community's conviction that the abbreviated Masoretic king law did not prevent the covenant violations of the historical monarchy — the full divine legislation including these provisions would have prevented the disasters documented in Melakhim.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11-12 — he shall not take another wife in addition to her — she alone shall be with him all the days of her life. The king's strict monogamy is the covenant model for all Yisra'EL. The Damascus Document Col 4-5 applies the same principle to all covenant members, grounding it in the creation order: Creator made one male and one female. The Temple Scroll grounds it in the royal covenant: the king who multiplies wives turns away his heart. The covenant family is one man and one woman — from king to commoner — because Creator's creation design is the standard for the covenant community.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 46 The King's Council — Twelve Priests and Twelve Levites and Twelve Princes — They Shall Sit With Him in Judgment — No Major Decision Without Their Counsel — The Covenant Governance

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 And you shall appoint for yourself twelve princes of the people and twelve priests and twelve Levites — they shall sit with the king as a council of governance.

2 And the king shall not do any major matter without their counsel — in all matters of war and peace and judgment he shall take counsel with them.

3 And the council of thirty-six shall be appointed from the wisest and most faithful men of each tribe and from the most learned of the priests and Levites.

4 And they shall serve for terms of years — no member shall serve indefinitely unless his faithfulness and wisdom remain beyond question.

5 And the council shall meet with the king at every new moon and at every feast — and at any time when a major matter arises requiring decision.

6 And no man shall serve on the council who has any blemish or any uncleanness — they shall be men of perfect covenant standing.

7 And the council shall examine any case of life and death before it is brought to the king — so that the king decides with full covenant wisdom and not alone.

8 And the priests on the council shall have authority over all matters of the Torah and its interpretation — no Torah ruling shall be made without the priests' confirmation.

9 And the Levites shall have authority over all matters of sanctuary and worship — their knowledge of the covenant service shall guide the king in all sacred matters.

10 And the princes shall have authority over all civil and military matters — they shall bring the wisdom of the twelve tribes to the king's governance.

11 And the decision of the council once reached shall be binding — the king shall act according to their counsel and shall not override it except in matters of immediate military necessity.

12 For the covenant requires that the king of Yisra'EL govern in the counsel of the covenant community — not as a sole ruler but as the head of the covenant governance — this is the law of the council.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 46 develops the royal council system introduced in Column 36. The thirty-six member council — twelve priests, twelve Levites, twelve tribal princes — mirrors the Qumran community's own governance structure as described in the Community Rule and the Damascus Document. The Community Rule describes a council of twelve men and three priests at the head of the community. The Temple Scroll's larger royal council applies the same principle of collective covenant governance to the national covenant community. No individual — not even the king — governs alone in the covenant system.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Shemot 18:13-27 — Yitro's advice to Mosheh about appointing judges — as the foundational model for collective covenant governance. The Orit Ge'ez also preserves Bamidbar 11:16-25 — the appointment of seventy elders to share the burden of governance — as the covenant precedent for collective leadership. The Temple Scroll's thirty-six member council draws on both traditions while applying them specifically to the royal governance context.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text has no direct parallel to the thirty-six member royal council. The Masoretic Devarim 17 king legislation does not specify a council. The Temple Scroll's council system is its most original contribution to covenant political philosophy — the king is embedded in a covenant governance structure that prevents the absolute monarchy that brought disaster to the historical kingdom. The council of priests, Levites, and tribal princes is the covenant check on royal power that the Masoretic king legislation only implied.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — the priests on the council shall have authority over all matters of the Torah and its interpretation. The priestly authority over Torah interpretation in the royal council reflects the Qumran community's core conviction that covenant governance must be grounded in correct covenant interpretation — and that correct interpretation belongs to the priestly office, not to popular opinion or royal decree. The Teacher of Righteousness exercised precisely this function in the community: the authoritative covenant interpreter whose rulings the community was bound to follow.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 47 The Military Organization — The Army of Yisra'EL — Its Commanders — Captains of Thousands and Hundreds and Fifties and Tens — The Covenant Warriors — Their Qualification

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 When you go out to war against your enemies and you see horses and chariots and an army larger than your own — you shall not be afraid of them — for YHWH your Creator is with you.

2 And when you draw near to the battle the priest shall come forward and speak to the people and shall say: Hear O Yisra'EL — you are drawing near today to do battle against your enemies.

3 Let not your heart faint — do not fear or panic or be in dread of them — for YHWH your Creator is he who goes with you to fight for you against your enemies — to give you the victory.

4 Then the officers shall speak to the people saying: Is there any man who has built a new house and has not dedicated it? Let him go back to his house lest he die in the battle.

5 Is there any man who has planted a vineyard and has not enjoyed its fruit? Let him go back to his house lest he die in the battle.

6 Is there any man who has betrothed a wife and has not taken her? Let him go back to his house lest he die in the battle.

7 And you shall appoint commanders over the army — commanders of thousands and commanders of hundreds and commanders of fifties and commanders of tens.

8 And at the head of all the commanders shall be the commander of the camp — he shall be chosen by the king with the counsel of the council from among the most capable warriors of Yisra'EL.

9 And the warriors of the covenant shall all be men who are faithful to the covenant — no man shall serve in the army of Yisra'EL who has transgressed any of the covenant statutes.

10 For the army of Yisra'EL is the army of YHWH — and YHWH will not go with an army that has uncleanness or covenant transgression in its midst.

11 And the priest who speaks to the warriors shall encourage them with the words of the covenant — he shall recite to them the covenant promises of victory and the covenant warnings of defeat through sin.

12 For the victory of Yisra'EL in battle belongs to YHWH — and he gives it to those who walk in his ways — this is the law of the covenant army.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 47 covers the military mustering legislation drawing on Devarim 20:1-9. The War Scroll (1QM — Vol 8 of this collection) develops the covenant military theology of Column 47 into its fullest expression — forty columns of detailed military organization, formations, weapons, and liturgy. Column 47 provides the Torah foundation; the War Scroll provides the eschatological elaboration. The two texts together constitute the most comprehensive covenant military theology in any ancient collection.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 20:1-9 — the military mustering speech — in full. The Orit Ge'ez also preserves the historical battle narratives of Yehoshua, Shofetim, and the Shemu'el books, which document the covenant military principles of Column 47 in historical operation. The Orit Ge'ez confirms that the battles of Yisra'EL are covenant events — won by Creator's intervention when the people are faithful, lost when they are not. Column 47's covenant military legislation grounds this historical pattern in Creator's direct covenant command.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 20:1-9 provides the base text for Column 47. The priest's speech to the warriors (verses 2-3), the four exemptions (verses 4-6), and the commander appointments (verse 7-8) all follow the Masoretic text closely. The Temple Scroll adds the covenant qualification requirement for all warriors (verse 9-10) — that only covenant-faithful men may serve in the army of YHWH — which has no explicit Masoretic parallel but is consistent with the War Scroll's requirement that all members of the covenant army be ritually and morally pure.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — YHWH will not go with an army that has uncleanness or covenant transgression in its midst. The covenant army's effectiveness depends entirely on Creator's presence and guidance. Creator's presence requires purity. Therefore covenant faithfulness is a military necessity, not merely a moral ideal. The Qumran community's War Scroll elaborates this principle into a comprehensive military theology: the messianic war at the end of days will be won by the covenant community not through military superiority but through covenant purity that enables Creator's direct intervention.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 48 The Treatment of Captive Cities — The Complete Destruction of Kena'anite Cities — You Shall Save Alive Nothing That Breathes — The Covenant Land Must Be Cleansed — The Distant Cities

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. 1 When you draw near to a city to fight against it offer it terms of peace — if it accepts your terms all the people in it shall do forced labor and serve you.

2 But if it does not make peace with you and makes war against you then you shall besiege it.

3 And when YHWH your Creator gives it into your hand you shall put all its males to the sword.

4 But the women and the little ones and the livestock and everything in the city — all its spoils — you may take for yourself.

5 And you shall enjoy the spoil of your enemies which YHWH your Creator has given you.

6 Thus shall you do to all the cities that are very far from you — which are not cities of these nations here.

7 But in the cities of these peoples that YHWH your Creator is giving you as an inheritance — you shall save alive nothing that breathes.

8 But you shall devote them to complete destruction — the Hittites and the Amorites and the Kena'anites and the Perizzites and the Hivites and the Yevusites — as YHWH your Creator has commanded.

9 That they may not teach you to do according to all their abominable practices that they have done for their gods — and so you would sin against YHWH your Creator.

10 When you besiege a city for a long time you shall not destroy its trees by wielding an axe against them — you may eat from them but you shall not cut them down.

11 Are the trees in the field men that they should be besieged by you? Only the trees that you know are not trees for food you may destroy and cut down.

12 For the trees of the field are the Creator's gift to the land — and the land is the Creator's covenant with Yisra'EL — you shall not destroy what Creator has given except what is required for the covenant war.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 48 covers the laws of warfare against distant cities and the complete-destruction command for the Kena'anite nations, drawing on Devarim 20:10-20. The Temple Scroll preserves the herem (complete destruction) command in Creator's direct voice — the most theologically difficult legislation in the covenant canon. The Qumran community understood the herem as the covenant purification of the land — the removal of the idolatrous practices that would corrupt the covenant community if allowed to remain. The tree preservation law of verses 10-12 softens the destruction mandate with an environmental covenant principle.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 20:10-20 — the siege and destruction laws — and the historical application of the herem in Yehoshua. The Orit Ge'ez preserves the tension between the herem command and its incomplete historical fulfillment: Yehoshua did not fully carry out the herem, and the Shofetim period documents the covenant consequences — the remaining Kena'anite peoples did teach Yisra'EL their abominable practices (verse 9). The Temple Scroll's herem command is thus both original covenant legislation and a response to the historical failure of its implementation.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 20:10-20 provides the base text for Column 48. The distinction between distant cities (verses 1-6) and the seven Kena'anite nations (verses 7-9) follows the Masoretic text precisely. The tree preservation law (verses 10-12) follows the Masoretic Devarim 20:19-20 exactly. The Temple Scroll preserves even the most difficult covenant legislation — the herem — in Creator's direct voice, refusing to soften or qualify what Creator has commanded, while providing the theological rationale: the Kena'anite practices must be removed lest they corrupt the covenant people.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — that they may not teach you to do according to all their abominable practices. The theological rationale for the herem is prophylactic covenant purity: the Kena'anite religious practices are incompatible with covenant life and will corrupt the covenant community if allowed to remain in the land. This is the same logic as the Temple Scroll's food laws and purity requirements: contamination spreads. The covenant land can only remain Creator's covenant sanctuary if all competing religious systems are removed. The herem is the most extreme expression of the covenant purity logic that runs through the entire Temple Scroll.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 49 The Priests in Battle — The Ark Goes Before the Army — The Trumpets of Assembly — The Return From Battle — Purification After Contact With the Dead — The Seven-Day Cleansing

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 When the army goes out to battle the priests shall go before them carrying the ark of the covenant — and the priests shall blow the trumpets of assembly before the army.

2 And when you hear the sound of the trumpets you shall remember YHWH your Creator who brought you up from the land of Egypt with a mighty hand.

3 And YHWH your Creator will go before you and fight for you against your enemies — and you shall see his salvation.

4 And after the battle when you return to the camp you shall purify yourselves from all the uncleanness of battle — from blood and from the dead.

5 Every man who has killed a person and every man who has touched a corpse shall be outside the camp seven days — he shall purify himself on the third day and the seventh day.

6 And all the garments and everything of skin and everything made of goat hair and every vessel of wood you shall purify on the third day and on the seventh day.

7 And on the seventh day he shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and at sunset he shall be clean — then he may return to the camp.

8 And the women taken captive shall be brought to the outer court of the sanctuary city — and they shall be purified there for thirty days before they may enter the city.

9 And all the metal spoils taken in battle shall be passed through fire to be clean — and all that cannot endure the fire shall be passed through the water of lustration.

10 And when the army has been purified you shall bring the offerings of thanksgiving before YHWH your Creator — burnt offerings and peace offerings for the victory he has given.

11 And the priests shall offer the covenant sacrifices and the warriors shall rejoice before YHWH in the sanctuary with all the congregation.

12 For the victory belongs to YHWH — and every victory of the covenant army shall be celebrated as a covenant thanksgiving before him in the place he has chosen — this is the law of the return from battle.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 49 covers the military-religious protocols for the ark in battle, the battle trumpets, and the post-battle purification. The War Scroll (1QM) develops all of these themes in extensive detail — the ark before the army (1QM Col 7), the trumpet system (1QM Col 2-3), and the post-battle purification liturgy. Column 49 establishes the foundational Torah legislation that the War Scroll elaborates into the eschatological military theology of the covenant community's final war against the Sons of Darkness.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Bamidbar 10:1-10 — the trumpets of assembly — and Bamidbar 31:19-24 — the post-battle purification — and Bamidbar 10:33-36 — the ark going before the people in the wilderness march. The Orit Ge'ez also preserves Yehoshua 6 — the ark circling Yericho — as the historical model for the ark's covenant military function. The Temple Scroll's priestly ark-bearers going before the army (verse 1) draws on all of these traditions as the established covenant model for the covenant army's march.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bamidbar 10 and 31 provide the primary base texts for Column 49. The seven-day post-battle purification (verse 5-7) follows Bamidbar 31:19-24 precisely. The thanksgiving offering requirement (verse 10) draws on the covenant principle that all victories belong to Creator and must be formally acknowledged through covenant sacrifice. The Temple Scroll systematically connects the military legislation with the sanctuary legislation: every act of the covenant army — before, during, and after battle — has its corresponding sanctuary ritual.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — the priests shall go before the army carrying the ark — and shall blow the trumpets. The ark going before the covenant army is the covenant declaration that the battle belongs to YHWH. The army follows the ark; the priests lead the warriors. This reversal of conventional military order — religious personnel at the front, warriors behind — is the covenant community's most radical military principle. Military success comes not from tactical superiority but from covenant proximity to Creator's presence. The ark is the physical symbol of that presence.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 50 The Covenant Renewal — The Assembly of All Yisra'EL — The Reading of the Torah — Every Seven Years at the Feast of Sukkot — That All May Hear and Learn and Fear YHWH

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Temple Scroll is the longest Dead Sea Scroll -- 66 columns -- found in Cave 11 at Qumran. It has never been appropriated by Christianity. No external tradition has claimed this text or used it to support false deity theology. The only concern noted is entirely internal to covenant scholarship: the Temple Scroll presents itself as direct divine speech -- Creator addressing Mosheh in the first person throughout all 66 columns. This is a strong claim of canonical authority that the Qumran community appears to have accepted at near-Torah level. The covenant content itself -- expanded festival laws, temple architecture, purity regulations, covenant statutes -- is entirely consistent with and grounded in the Mosaic Torah tradition. There is nothing in this text that a covenant-faithful reader should be alarmed by. Read this text as what it is: the most extensive surviving covenant legal document from Qumran -- pure Torah-rooted covenant legislation with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 At the end of every seven years at the appointed time in the year of release at the Feast of Sukkot when all Yisra'EL comes to appear before YHWH your Creator at the place he will choose —

2 you shall read this Torah before all Yisra'EL in their hearing — assemble the people: men, women and little ones, and the sojourner who is within your towns.

3 That they may hear and learn and fear YHWH your Creator and be careful to do all the words of this Torah.

4 And that their children who have not known it may hear and learn to fear YHWH your Creator as long as you live in the land that you are crossing the Jordan to possess.

5 And the king shall stand before all the assembly and read from the Torah in their hearing — from the first day to the last day.

6 He shall not exalt himself above the reading — he shall read as a covenant member reading the covenant word — not as a king pronouncing decrees.

7 And the priests shall stand with him as he reads — they shall correct him if he errs — for the accuracy of the covenant word is more important than the dignity of the king.

8 And all the people shall listen with their whole heart and their whole soul — for the word of the Torah is the word of Creator and it shall not return void.

9 And after the reading the king shall make covenant renewal before all the congregation — he shall swear before YHWH to uphold all the words of the Torah all the days of his life.

10 And after the king the council of thirty-six shall renew the covenant — and after them the priests and the Levites — and after them all the congregation of Yisra'EL.

11 And YHWH your Creator will confirm the covenant with those who have renewed it — their names shall be written in the book of the covenant before him.

12 For the covenant renewal is the most important act of the covenant year — greater than all the festivals — for it is the personal oath of every member of the covenant people before YHWH — this is the law of the hakhel.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 50 covers the hakhel — the septennial Torah reading assembly — drawing on Devarim 31:10-13. The Community Rule's annual covenant renewal ceremony is the Qumran community's more frequent adaptation of the septennial hakhel: rather than waiting seven years, the community renews the covenant annually at Shavuot. Column 50's provision that the priests may correct the king if he errs in reading (verse 7) is the Temple Scroll's most striking subordination of royal to priestly authority: even at the most public covenant event, the priestly covenant knowledge takes precedence over royal dignity.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 31:10-13 — the hakhel legislation — and the Orit Ge'ez Nekhemyah 8 — the historical implementation of the public Torah reading under Ezra and Nekhemyah. The Orit Ge'ez Nekhemyah 8 is the most detailed historical account of public Torah reading in the entire covenant historical literature — the entire community gathers, Ezra reads from the Torah, the Levites explain it to the people, and the people weep when they hear the words. The Temple Scroll's Column 50 provides the legislation; Nekhemyah 8 provides the historical model.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 31:10-13 provides the base text for Column 50. The Temple Scroll significantly expands the Masoretic legislation with the covenant renewal ceremony (verses 9-11) and the book of the covenant registration (verse 11). The septennial hakhel was the most inclusive covenant event in the Torah legislation — all Yisra'EL, including women, children, and sojourners, were required to hear the Torah. The Temple Scroll preserves this radical inclusivity while adding the formal covenant renewal that transforms the hearing into a covenant commitment.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — the covenant renewal is the most important act of the covenant year — greater than all the festivals. The Temple Scroll's elevation of the covenant renewal above all the festivals is its most striking liturgical ranking. The Yom Kippur is the holiest day; the covenant renewal is the most important act. This distinction reflects the Temple Scroll's understanding that the covenant relationship is prior to and more fundamental than any specific covenant observance. The festivals express and celebrate the covenant; the renewal is the covenant itself being re-enacted.

TEMPLE SCROLL — COLUMN 51 The Sanctuary of the Camp — The Three Camps — The Camp of YHWH — The Camp of the Priests — The Camp of Yisra'EL — Their Purity Requirements — The Distance of the Latrines

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 17, 19, 21, and 25 — all the base texts for Column 57. 11 Beware lest you say in your heart: My power and the might of my hand have gotten me this wealth — for you shall remember YHWH your Creator for it is he who gives you power to get wealth.

12 And if you forget YHWH your Creator and go after other gods and serve them and worship them — I solemnly warn you today that you shall surely perish — as the nations that YHWH makes to perish before you so shall you perish because you would not obey the voice of YHWH your Creator.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 63 draws on the Shema (Devarim 6:4-5) and the surrounding covenant summary passage (Devarim 6:1-15) — the most central covenant text in the entire Mosaic tradition. The Shema is the covenant community's daily confession: YHWH is our Creator, YHWH is one, and we shall love him with all our heart and soul and might. The Community Rule requires the twice-daily recitation of the Shema (1QS 10:10) — Column 63's covenant legislation grounds this community practice in direct divine command.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 6:1-15 — the covenant summary and Shema — in full. The Orit Ge'ez Ethiopian covenant community has maintained the twice-daily Shema recitation as a foundational covenant practice throughout its existence. The Shema of Column 63 verse 5 is the covenant community's most concentrated declaration: YHWH alone is Creator, YHWH is uniquely one, and the covenant response is total love — heart, soul, might. Nothing is held back from the covenant Creator.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 6:1-15 provides the complete base text for Column 63. The Shema (Devarim 6:4-5) is the most frequently cited covenant text in the entire Hebrew tradition and appears here in Creator's own direct voice — I command you this covenant declaration. The mezuzah requirement (verse 8) — write them on the doorposts of your house — is the covenant community's physical marking of their dwelling spaces as covenant spaces. The Temple Scroll preserves the Shema and its surrounding covenant summary as the theological heart of the entire covenant legal program.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — Hear O Yisra'EL: YHWH is our Creator — YHWH is one. The Shema is the covenant community's anti-idolatry declaration: YHWH alone is Creator — not one among many, not first among equals — but uniquely one. Every other claim to divine status is false by definition. This declaration grounds the entire covenant legal program of the Temple Scroll: all the festivals, the purity laws, the sanctuary architecture, the judicial system, the royal legislation — all of it flows from the single covenant reality that YHWH alone is Creator and that his people are bound to him exclusively in love.

2 A people great and tall — the sons of the Anakim — whom you know and of whom you have heard it said: Who can stand before the sons of Anak?

3 Know therefore today that he who goes over before you as a consuming fire is YHWH your Creator — he will destroy them and subdue them before you.

4 Do not say in your heart after YHWH your Creator has thrust them out before you: It is because of my righteousness that YHWH has brought me in to possess this land.

5 For it is not because of your righteousness or the uprightness of your heart that you are going in to possess their land — but because of the wickedness of these nations YHWH your Creator is driving them out.

6 Know therefore that it is not because of your righteousness that YHWH your Creator is giving you this good land — for you are a stubborn people.

7 Remember and do not forget how you provoked YHWH your Creator to wrath in the wilderness — from the day you came out of Egypt until you came to this place you have been rebellious against YHWH.

9 But I pleaded with YHWH your Creator and fell down before YHWH — forty days and forty nights I lay prostrate.

COMPARATIVE NOTES

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 64 draws on Devarim 9:1-13 and 10:12-13 — the covenant warning against self-righteousness and the summary covenant requirement. The emphasis on Yisra'EL's historical stubbornness (verses 7-10) and Creator's mercy despite that stubbornness is the covenant theological foundation for the restoration hope of Column 44: Creator's faithfulness to the covenant is not conditional on Yisra'EL's perfection but on his own covenant commitment. The community at Qumran — who saw themselves as a remnant among a stubbornly apostate people — would have found this passage particularly significant.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 9 and 10 in full — the covenant warning against self-righteousness and the covenant summary. The Hodayot (Vol 40) contains the Teacher's own prayers of intercession modeled on Mosheh's Horev intercession.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 9:1-13 and 10:12-13 provide the base texts for Column 64. The self-righteousness warning — it is not because of your righteousness (verses 4-6) — is the Masoretic Torah's most direct covenant theological correction of human pride. The covenant people possess the land because of the nations' wickedness and Creator's promise to the patriarchs — not because of any merit of their own. This anti-meritocratic covenant theology is the foundation on which the Hodayot's radical anthropological humility is built: no human is righteous before Creator; all covenant standing is gift.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — what does YHWH your Creator require of you — but to fear YHWH your Creator, to walk in all his ways, to love him, to serve YHWH your Creator with all your heart and with all your soul. The covenant summary of Devarim 10:12-13 is the Temple Scroll's most concise statement of the entire covenant: fear, walk, love, serve — four covenant actions that encompass all the detailed legislation of the sixty-six columns. All the sanctuary specifications, purity laws, festivals, judicial systems, and royal legislation are the unpacking of these four covenant actions in their concrete lived reality.

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 65 draws on Devarim 31:7-28 — the final charge to Yehoshua and the writing of the Torah as witness. Placing this scroll beside the ark would be placing Creator's own direct revelation in the most sacred covenant location — beside the tablets of the covenant. The Temple Scroll's self-understanding as a covenant document of the highest authority is here expressed at its most explicit.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 31:7-28 in full — the final charge, the writing of the Torah, and its placement beside the ark. The Orit Ge'ez Yehoshua 1 — the divine charge to Yehoshua to be strong and courageous — continues the covenant leadership theme of Column 65 into the beginning of the Nevi'im. Column 65 bridges the Torah legislation and the Nevi'im narrative: the Torah is written, the covenant is sealed, the land conquest begins.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Devarim 31:7-28 provides the base text for Column 65. The writing of the Torah and its placement beside the ark (verses 6-9) is the Torah's own account of its canonization — the written covenant document given permanent covenant status beside the physical covenant objects (the tablets in the ark). The Temple Scroll claims this canonical status for itself: it is the Torah that Creator wrote and that must be placed beside the ark. The scroll's self-presentation as direct divine speech throughout sixty-five columns culminates in this claim to canonical placement beside the covenant's most sacred objects.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — in the day of your return my covenant will be waiting for you. The final theological statement before Column 66's closing is the covenant's most enduring promise: Creator's covenant does not expire. Even after the predicted rebellion (verse 4) — even after the predicted exile (Column 43) — the covenant remains. When the stubborn people finally return (Column 44's restoration), the covenant will be waiting exactly as it was left. The Temple Scroll's legislation is not merely the covenant for the wilderness generation or for the pre-exile period: it is the eternal covenant that will be implemented in full when the covenant people finally return.

[DSS] 11Q19 Column 66 is the closing column of the Temple Scroll — the final summary and sealing of the covenant. The final verse (verse 12) is the Temple Scroll's most comprehensive summary: this is the Torah of the temple — keep it and do it — that I may dwell among you forever. [ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Devarim 8 and 30 — the good land description and the covenant choice — which provide the base texts for Column 66. The Orit Ge'ez prophetic tradition consistently grounds the covenant community's hope in the permanence of Creator's word: the historical disasters and exiles cannot permanently override what Creator has spoken. The Temple Scroll's final verse (verse 12) is the scroll's own self-description — this is the Torah of the temple — which has no Masoretic parallel. The Torah of the temple is not an end in itself. It is the means to the covenant community's deepest reality: Emmanuel — Creator with us — forever.
